State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
:File 
Date 
october 3, 1988 
From 
: 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
John G. McLean 
Subject:Advice Request No. T-88-365 
Spoke today with Mary Hansen. 
Advised her that under emergency 
Regulation 18901, the Mayor's column may not be included in a mass 
mailing. 
Further advised her that the newsletter may not contain 
the name, signature, photograph, or any other reference to an 
elected officer. 
JGM:ld:T88365 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Mary Getty Hansen 
Town Clerk 
142 Bolinas Road 
Fairfax, CA 
94930 
Dear Ms. Hansen: 
September 21, 1988 
Re: 
88-365 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on September 20, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact John McLean, an attorney in the 
Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329).) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

California Cities

TO: 
FROM: 
RE: 
League of California Cities 
1400 K STREET • SACRAMENTO. CA 95814 • (916) 444-5790 
AUGUST 
1988 
All city Attorneys -
Paul Valle-Riestra, Staff Attorney 
Newsletters and Other Mass Mailings 
Proposition 73 amended Section 89001 of the Government Code to 
read, "No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent at 
public expense." 
Recently we sent to you an advice letter from the Fair Political 
Practices commission (FPPC) dated July 1, concerning mass 
mailings. 
On July 26, the FPPC adopted an emergency regulation on mass 
mailings, a copy of which is attached. 
The emergency regulation 
appears to follow the advice letter. 
Note that the advice letter 
at page 5 states that a newsletter may contain a standard 
roster-type listing of elected officials. 
While the emergency 
regulation does not specifically authorize this listing, FPPC 
staff has told us that these listings are still permitted. 
However, you may wish to request an advice letter on this subject 
before including such a listing in a newsletter. 
One of the Commissioners questioned whether they have the legal 
authority to permit certain mass mailings when the text of 
Proposition 73 appears to prohibit publicly funded mass mailings 
across the board. 
FPPC staff gave the preliminary opinion that 
they do have this authority. 
However, the FPPC directed staff to 
prepare a legal analysis of this authority for the september 
meeting. 
At that meeting, the FPPC is expected to pre-notice a 
permanent regulation on mass mailings for consideration at the 
November meeting.

PUBLISHED BY THE TOWN OF FAIRFAX 
Message from the Mayor 
Uf?lcome to the second issue of the Fairfax Newsletter. Just 
about a year ago, a questionnaire was inserted in our Town 
Newsletter and we asked Fairfax residents to comment on the 
Town's assets and liabilities, the downtown, housing and 
transportation issues. 
Over three hundred questionnaires were returned to us, which 
\1.US a 7.5% response, considered quite good when using the mail 
back survey technique. The results were of great interest to the 
Council, so we(f like to spend a large part of the Newsletter shar-
ing those responses with you so you will know how your fellow 
residents feel on a variety of subjects. 
Uf? hope you enjoy reading this issue of the Newsletter and 
we thank everyone who took the time to respond to our survey. 
As a public organization, our single purpose is to serve, main-
tain, improve and protect the community and we welcome your 
comments about the services we provide. Let us know how we're 
doing. 
FRONT 
NOVEMBER - DECEMBER, 1987 
Parks and Recreation Commission 
Seeks Your Ideas 
The Fairfax Parks and Recreation Commission is planning for 
the future and seeks your ideas regarding its role in the Com-
munity. The Commission is involved in programs such as the 
Annual Fairfax Festival, the Septemberfest and Christmas pro-
grams and most recently, the completion of Cascade Park restora-
tion, the upgrading of Peri Park Playground and the creation 
of the Fairfax Youth Center. 
At present, the Parks and Recreation Commission is discuss-
ing such options as offering traditional recreational program-
ming which could include adult volleyball, basketball, sports 
programs, after-school playground programs and specialized 
classes, all to be supported by self-sustaining fees. In addition, 
the Commission could expand its emphasis and provide more 
cultural events and activities, promoting the rich heritage 
of the Town. 
The Commission would like to hear your ideas and sugges-
tions. Write or call Town Hall, and remember that the Fairfax 
Parks and Recreation Commission meets the fourth Thursday 
of each month at the Fairfax Women's Club, 7:30 P.M. 
SIDE 
Bandstand Proposed For Bolinas Road Park 
A group of private citizens have proposed a unique addition to the Bolinas Road park where the Fairfax Festival is held each 
year. Plans are in the works to create a non-profit organization to raise money for construction of an old fashion bandstand. The 
town budgeted $2,000 as seed money for this effort. This will be a fun project which will benefit the entire community. The goal 
is to have the bandstand in place for a summer concert series in 1988. For details call Robert Kennedy, 457-3847. 


Peri Park-
Many hours 
were spent 
installing 
the new 
equipment! 
Improvements for Peri Park 
Using State Bond Act monies, Peri Park has received a $24,000 
face lift. New equipment, sand, benches, trash containers and 
a walkway are in place. The improvements have proven to be 
a big hit with the Fairfax younger set and their parents. The 
Friends of Peri Park, a volunteer group of parents and concerned 
citizens, spearheaded the drive to upgrade the park area, and 
spent many hours installing the equipment. The entire Town can 
be proud of the final product. 
New Look For Sir Francis Drake Boulevard 
And Other Improvement Projects 
Undergrounding utility lines and the planting of twenty-two 
new street trees along Sir Francis Drake Boulevard will result 
in an open and more attractive vista in the area from 2000 Sir 
Francis Drake west to Marin Road. The majority of work is com-
pleted and the trees will be planted in January once all the utili-
ty lines are removed. The trees were paid for with Town funds, 
but the cost of the $350,000 Undergrounding Project was almost 
entirely funded by PG&E. 
Also soon to be installed will be four hundred feet of new safe-
ty guardrail on Sir Francis Drake Boulevard on either side of 
Marin Road. The estimated cost is $44,000, but because of a 
State Grant, the Town's share will be only 10% of that cost. 
Also slated for the summer of 1988 will be completion of the 
repaving of Marinda Drive, at an estimated cost of $44,000. 
Another project planned will be funded jointly by the Town 
and the Golden Gate Transit District. It includes construction 
of a sidewalk between the stairs and the handicapped ramp. This 
will provide greater safety and convenience for commuters get-
ting off the bus in the evening. 
Typesetting, Graphic Camera Work & Production 
Van Norman/Associates

Fairfax, California 94930

U.S. Postage

be found at the lawn area outside Town Hall from 12 P.M. 
to 3 P.M. these same days. 
Call Santa on December 21,22 and 23, from 6 to 8 P.M. 
at 453-1587

PUBLISHED BY THE TOWN OF FAIRFAX 
Message From The Mayor 
Welcome to the first issue of the Fairfax Newsletter! Because 
the Council believes that communication with all of our residents 
is a priority we have undertaken this little venture, knowing full 
well that the Newsletter will never pose a threat to the established 
press of Marin County. However, there are interesting sidelights 
about your town government we would like to share with you, 
as well as being able to keep you informed of our goals and 
policies as they are developed. 
We would like to hear from you; are there issues you would 
like more fully explained, objectives that need to be more clearly 
defined, and most ()f all, do you like the Newsletter? 
~~ 
Will You Volunteer for Fairfax? 
The strength of a local community builds on the willingness 
of the people who live in it to help take care of each other; watch-
ing out fur children and teenagers, keeping parks safe and clean, 
taking care of shut-ins, having fun together like the Fairfax 
Festival and block parties. Volunteering is one way we take care 
of each other. However we often do not know who needs help 
or what needs to be done. Some kind of local clearing-house 
such as the San Anselmo Volunteer Effort funded by the town 
could help us in Fairfax. 
There are some people in Fairfax who would like to get 
together in the New Year, Wednesday, January 14, at 7:30 P.M., 
at the Fairfax Women's Club, to discuss the needs of Fairfax 
and to see if we can get a Fairfax Volunteer Program up and 
going. Part of the agenda will be devoted to discussing if having 
a Town Meeting in 1987 can happen. People who are interested 
should contact Michael Maeder at 457-5723. 
DECEMBER, 1986 
Your Town and Its Government 
Fairfax has a population of approximately 7,400 in a 2.2 square 
mile area, with 27 miles of roads. We have three schools and 
four churches. 
. 
Located at 142 Bolinas Road, along with the Police Depart-
ment, the Town Hall is open from 8:30 A.M. to 5 P.M. Mon-
day through Thursday, and 8:30 A.M. to noon on Fridays. Our 
phone number is 453-1584. The Police Department is open 24 
hours a day. The Fire Department, run by the Ross Valley Fire 
Service, is around the corner on Park Road. 
The Town has twenty-nine employees: seventeen in the police 
department, six in streets and park maintenance, and six in 
administration, finance, building and planning. 
There is a five member Town Council who are elected to of-
fice for four year terms. The Council appoints a Town Ad-
ministrator to carry out its policies, goals and objectives. 
Councilmembers include Mayor Carol Sherman, Vice-Mayor 
Gloria Duncan, Frank Egger, Ken Lippi and Wendy Baker. 
Key staff members are: 
Charles R. Cate, Town Administrator 
Walt Potter, Chief of Police 
Bill Street, Superintendent of Streets 
Nancy M. Marion, Director of Community Development 
Mary Getty Hansen, Town Clerk 
We welcome your comments on how the Town can improve 
its public service. Do not hesitate to call us. 
ON BEHALF OF THE TOWN COUNCIL AND STAFF, 
WE WOULD LIKE TO WISH YOU ALL A VERY HAPPY 
AND HEALTHY HOLIDAY SEASON. 
The old Fairfax 1929 Seagrave Fire Engine recently restored thanks to the Fire I#1rden's Club. 


Update on 1986 Storm Damage Construction Projects 
The Town was hit hard last February with road and hillside slipout problems that required major repair 
work. Construction projects to repair damage have been completed on upper Scenic across from 484, 
lower Scenic across from 355, and at the end of Pine Drive. One other major repair remains at 223 
Scenic and that contract is currently under way. We appreciate the patience of the residents of Manor 
Hill during the construction period. 
Fixing last year's damage on Scenic Road. 
Parking Enforcement Program Under Way 
Under the direction of Chief Walt Potter, the Fairfax Police 
Department is taking a fair, but consistent, approach to enforce~ 
ment of the Town's parking regulations. There are two code sec-
tions which can be particularly hazardous if not enforced, 
especially in the hillside areas of Town. You can help by: 
• Curbing, blocking or turning wheels into a bank, and 
• Parking to leave a minimum of 12 I clearance on the roadway. 
Building Permits and Public Safety 
The Town has a full service building department which is com-
mitted to assisting the public in the processing and approval of 
construction related permits and inspecting work in progress. 
The construction codes are intended to safeguard the public 
against unsafe building practices and materials. The majority 
of construction, including reconstruction and repairs, require 
securing a building permit from the Town. If you have doubts 
as to whether a building permit is required, please contact the 
Building/Planning Department at 453-1584 during Town Hall 
business hours, prior to commencing any work. ~ 
will be most 
happy to answer your questions and assist you in any way we can. 
Typesetting & Production -
Van Norman/Associates 
Postal Customer 
BULK RATE 
U.S. Postage 
PAID 
Fairfax, CA 
Permit #43 
Fairfax, California 94930 
Happy Holidays!

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city may publish a newsletter containing a 'Message from the Mayor' without violating Government Code Section 89001's prohibition on mass mailings sent at public expense, particularly regarding inclusion of the Mayor's message and elected officials' references.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under emergency Regulation 18901, the Mayor's column may not be included in a mass mailing. The newsletter may not contain the name, signature, photograph, or any other reference to an elected officer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both formal letter of receipt dated September 21, 1988, and informal memorandum dated October 3, 1988 documenting the actual advice given. The question is reconstructed from the incoming request letter from Mary Hansen (Town Clerk). Document also includes related regulatory materials and newsletter samples. Some pages partially damaged/illegible but key advice clearly stated in McLean memo.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a city may publish a newsletter containing a 'Message from the Mayor' without violating Government Code Section 89001's prohibition on mass mailings sent at public expense, particularly regarding inclusion of the Mayor's message and elected officials' references.

CONCLUSION: Under emergency Regulation 18901, the Mayor's column may not be included in a mass mailing. The newsletter may not contain the name, signature, photograph, or any other reference to an elected officer.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry from Town Clerk Mary Hansen regarding whether the Town of Fairfax may publish a city newsletter with a Mayor's message under Proposition 73's ban on mass mailings sent at public expense. FPPC advised that the Mayor's column cannot be included in mass mailings and newsletters cannot reference elected officers.
